text: Laparoscopic robotic-assisted Nissen takedown with Heller myotomy and door funduplication for achalasia. We present the case of a sixty-nine-year-old male with history of laparoscopic Nissen funduplication ten years ago, who presents with progressive dysphagia and one kilogram weight lost for one year.

Preoperative studies were performed. An EGT showed spasticity of the esophageal body. A motility study did not evidence any change in the lower esophageal sphincter pressure in response to swallows, nor was esophageal peristalsis detected. Also, esophageal pressures appear to persist across the GE junction. These findings were in accordance to spastic esophageal motor disorder. Preoperative upper GI contrast study re-demonstrated diffuse esophageal dysmotility with smooth tapering at the distal esophagus, which may be related to type III achalasia.

Trochars are placed in the abdominal wall for the procedure. The funduplication is completed, and an intraoperative endoscopy is performed, confirming asthenosis at the gastroesophageal junction due to achalasia. The surgeon then proceeds with a laparoscopic robotic-assisted Heller myotomy followed by Thor funduplication.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the hiatus, revealing lax adhesions between the liver, stomach, and the previous Nissen fundoplication wrap. A hiatal hernia is observed, with the Nissen wrap partially slipped into the chest cavity.

The surgeon performs a gentle circumferential dissection of the hiatus, releasing adhesions between the stomach, diaphragm, and caudate lobe of the liver. Short gastric vessels are divided, and a retrogastric window is created to pass a Penrose drain, aiding in further dissection.

The surgeon dissects the mediastinum to identify the esophagus, performing a circumferential dissection of the esophagus extending at least fifteen centimeters into the chest. The Nissen funduplication is then repositioned back into the abdomen along with the gastroesophageal junction, and the funduplication is taken down.
An intraoperative endoscopy reveals a significantly dilated esophagus and a constricted gastroesophageal junction, visually consistent with achalasia.

A 48-tapered bougie is introduced into the esophagus to guide the myotomy. The surgeon uses electrocautery and monopolar scissors to perform the myotomy, transecting both longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the esophagus for five centimeters into the chest.

The surgeon transects the transverse oblique and circular muscle layers at the gastroesophageal junction and gastric portion of the myotomy, measuring approximately three centimeters. The Bauhaus nerve is carefully preserved during this process.
An intraoperative endoscopy is performed, showing no evidence of leaks. The endoscope smoothly traverses the gastroesophageal junction without difficulty.

The surgeon approximates the pillars of the hiatus using interrupted stitches to restore anatomical structure. Several stitches are placed between the left and right grooves on the same side of the myotomy to maintain its openness.
The surgeon flips the stomach over the myotomy using the door technique, completing the Dorfung duplication. Hemostasis is checked to ensure there is no bleeding.

Postoperative upper GI contrast study showed resolution of esophageal dilation with prompt passage of contrast through the GI junction and no evidence of leaks. The patient was asymptomatic during the postoperative course and she was discharged on postoperative day four.
